title: A form that actually works
statement:
An empty editor. Build the form from the target on the right.

Nothing here shows on screen. A field looks the same whether it is tied to its label or not. Tied — a blind person knows what goes in it. Not tied — they hear only an empty box.
constraints: Group About you: Age, Country (at least three countries), Tell us about yourself
Every field has its own label, tied to it
Every field is of the right type: email, password, number

checks.t4: There are at least three fields
checks.t6: There is a large text field
checks.t7: The fields are tied to their labels
checks.t9: The email field is of type email
checks.t10: The password field is of type password
checks.t11: The age field is of type number
checks.t12: The dropdown has at least three choices

why.empty: The editor is empty. There is no starter code here — you build the form yourself.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed. Input fields have no closing tag, but everything else does.
why.no-heading: There is no heading, or it is empty.
why.no-form: The fields are there, but they are not inside a form. The form is what collects them and sends them together.
why.few-fields: There are not three input fields. Email, password and age make three.
why.no-select: There is no dropdown. A country is not typed freely — it is picked from a ready list.
why.no-textarea: There is no large text field. Telling us about yourself does not fit on one line.
why.no-labels:
There is a field with no label tied to it. You wrote the word next to the field — a sighted person sees it, and that fools you into thinking everything is fine.

But a label is not text next to a field. It is a LINK. A blind person focuses the field and the reader says nothing useful: "edit box, blank". They do not know what the page is asking for.
why.wrong-types: There is a field of the wrong type. All fields look the same — a rectangle. But the type decides whether a phone shows a letter keyboard or a number one, whether the password is hidden, whether the browser offers the saved email.
why.few-options: The dropdown has fewer than three choices.
why.no-groups: The fields are not grouped. Six fields in a row are a wall. Groups say what belongs with what.
why.no-legend: The group has no name, or it is empty. A group without a name is a box without a label — you know things are collected together, but not what they are.
why.no-button: There is no button. The form can be filled in, but it cannot be sent.

no-labels: The label and the field are tied through a shared name. One points, the other is called that.
There are two correct ways. Either the label points at the field's name, or the label wraps the field entirely. Both work.
Check each field separately: email, password, age, country, story. Five fields, five links. One broken link is enough.
wrong-types: An input field has an attribute that says what is expected inside.
Without it, the field is plain text. Three of your fields are not plain text.
Email, password and number are three different types. Each has its own name.
no-groups: There is a tag that fences off several fields and says: these belong together.
Each group wants a name too — the first thing inside it.
not-closed: Input fields have no closing tag. Everything else does.
The groups, the form, the labels, the large text field — each of those closes.

solution.why:
The first instinct is to write the word and put the field next to it. On screen it looks like a form — and it works, if you have eyes.

But a word next to a field is not a label. It is just text standing nearby. A blind person tabs to the field and the reader says: "edit box, blank". Nothing more. They do not know whether an email is wanted, a password, or an age.

The second omission is the type. Every field looks the same — a white rectangle. But the type decides whether a phone shows a number keyboard, whether the password is hidden, whether the browser offers the saved address.
steps: Open the form. It collects everything and sends it together.
Split the fields into two groups and name each one. Six fields in a row are a wall.
For every field write the label BEFORE it and tie the two through a shared name.
Give every field the right type: email, password, number. Without a type a field is plain text.
The country is not a typing field — it is a dropdown with ready choices.
The story is not one line — it is a large text field.
Finally a button. Without it the form fills in but goes nowhere.
solution.code:
Sign up

Account
About you
solution.note: A form is where the gap between "looks right" and "is right" is widest. Everything here is invisible: the links, the types, the groups. Run it and look — you will not see a single one of the mistakes you just fixed.
